subject: ap-world-history
theme: Between 1750 and 1900, European industrialization fueled imperial expansion across Asia, Africa, and the Pacific, producing extractive colonial economies, new global labor systems, and ideological justifications that linked racial hierarchy to the 'civilizing mission' — while mass migration reshaped demographic patterns and sparked indigenous resistance worldwide.

core_concepts.title: New Imperialism & Scramble for Africa
core_concepts.detail: European industrialization created intense demand for raw materials and new markets, driving the 'New Imperialism' of the 19th century in which European states rapidly claimed colonial territories across Africa, Asia, and Oceania — culminating in the Berlin Conference (1884–85) which formalized the 'Scramble for Africa' by dividing the continent among European powers without African consent.

core_concepts.title: Social Darwinism & Civilizing Mission
core_concepts.detail: Ideological justifications for imperialism drew on Social Darwinism, which applied evolutionary 'survival of the fittest' logic to human societies to argue that European domination of non-European peoples was scientifically inevitable, and the 'civilizing mission' / 'White Man's Burden,' which framed colonial extraction as a moral duty to bring education, Christianity, and modern institutions to 'uncivilized' peoples.

core_concepts.title: Forms of Imperial Control
core_concepts.detail: Imperialism operated through multiple mechanisms: direct colonial rule (as in British India after 1858), settler colonialism (as in Australia, New Zealand, and Algeria where European migrants displaced indigenous populations), and economic imperialism (as in China after the Opium Wars, where spheres of influence gave foreign powers economic rights without formal annexation).

core_concepts.title: Company Rule to State Administration
core_concepts.detail: The transition from company rule to direct state administration — exemplified by Britain's assumption of control over India from the East India Company after the 1857 Revolt — reflected a general pattern in which colonial enterprises became too large and politically sensitive for private companies to manage, requiring formal government authority.

core_concepts.title: Indigenous Resistance to Colonialism
core_concepts.detail: Colonialism generated diverse forms of indigenous resistance: military resistance (Ethiopian victory at Adwa 1896; Zulu resistance in South Africa), political resistance (Indian National Congress founded 1885), and mass rebellions (Indian Revolt of 1857 / Sepoy Mutiny; Boxer Rebellion in China 1899–1901; Taiping Rebellion 1850–64).

core_concepts.title: Global Migration & Indentured Labor
core_concepts.detail: Global migration flows transformed demographic patterns in the late 19th century: the abolition of chattel slavery created demand for new labor systems, producing large-scale indentured servitude (moving millions from South Asia to the Caribbean, Fiji, South Africa, and Malaya), while economic immigration created ethnic enclaves (Chinese communities in California and Southeast Asia, Indian communities in East Africa).

core_concepts.title: Racist Immigration Restriction Laws
core_concepts.detail: Racist immigration restrictions — including the White Australia Policy, the Chinese Exclusion Act (US, 1882), and similar laws in Canada and South Africa — reflected how receiving societies attempted to limit the racial diversity that global labor migration was creating, demonstrating the intersection of imperialism, racism, and migration policy.

exam_tip: Unit 6 MCQs emphasize causation: the AP exam frequently asks why European imperialism expanded so rapidly in this period (industrialization, technology, ideological justification) and what effects it had on colonized societies (economic dependency, social disruption, cultural resistance). Practice distinguishing between the different forms of imperial control (settler colonies vs. exploitation colonies vs. economic imperialism), and be prepared to analyze primary sources that express both the ideology of imperialism and indigenous resistance perspectives. Sourcing skills (point of view, purpose, audience) are heavily tested in this unit.
